input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin system** in the provided documents primarily pertains to **academic infrastructure digital twins**, particularly focusing on **LEED Platinum-certified buildings** (e.g., the case study in Document [33]) and **university library indoor environmental monitoring** (Document [34]). These systems are designed for **research-oriented applications**, integrating **sensors, IoT devices, and real-time data streams** to simulate and analyze physical conditions such as temperature, humidity, air quality, and occupancy dynamics.

  **Key Components**:
  - **Sensors & IoT Devices**: Deployed in living labs and academic buildings to monitor environmental parameters (e.g., thermal, acoustic, and energy-related metrics).
  - **Living Labs Framework**: A structured environment (e.g., Document [33]) where digital twins are developed and validated through iterative research cycles, leveraging **LEED certification principles** for sustainability and performance optimization.
  - **Indoor Condition Monitoring**: Specifically addressed in Document [34] for university libraries, emphasizing **real-time data collection** for occupancy, lighting, and HVAC system efficiency.
  - **Operational Domain**: Primarily **academic/research facilities**, with applications in **energy management, occupant comfort, and facility maintenance**, though the principles could extend to **commercial buildings** with similar certifications (e.g., LEED).

  **Technical Suitability for Digital Twinning**:
  - High **data density** due to sensor-rich environments (e.g., thousands of IoT nodes in Document [34] for library monitoring).
  - Emphasis on **model-driven validation** (Documents [35], [36], [37], [38]) to ensure alignment between physical and digital representations.
  - Support for **real-time synchronization** via **IIoT (Industrial Internet of Things) protocols** (implied in Document [31] for basyx platform integration).
  - Focus on **energy efficiency and sustainability metrics**, aligning with **model-based systems engineering (MBSE)** methodologies (Document [38]).
  - Use of **living labs** as a controlled testbed for iterative digital twin development (Document [33]), enabling **A/B testing** of system configurations.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The Digital Twin services documented are tailored to **research-oriented academic buildings** and **library indoor condition monitoring**, with a focus on:
  - **Monitoring**:
    - Real-time **environmental parameter tracking** (temperature, humidity, CO₂ levels, occupancy) via **IoT sensors** (Document [34]).
    - **Predictive analytics** for occupancy forecasting (implied in Document [33] for LEED-certified buildings).
    - **Energy consumption profiling** to optimize HVAC and lighting systems (Document [33]).
  - **Optimization**:
    - **Model-driven tuning** of physical systems (e.g., Document [35] on model-driven engineering) to improve energy efficiency or occupant comfort.
    - **Dynamic adjustment** of building controls (e.g., HVAC zoning, ventilation rates) based on real-time data (Document [33]).
  - **Prediction**:
    - **Occupancy prediction** for library spaces (Document [34]) to optimize resource allocation (e.g., lighting, seating).
    - **Failure prediction** for critical infrastructure (e.g., HVAC systems) using historical and real-time data (implied in Document [33]).
  - **Control**:
    - **Automated actuation** of building systems (e.g., smart thermostats, automated lighting) via **digital twin-driven protocols** (Document [31] for basyx platform integration).
    - **Remote diagnostics** for maintenance scheduling (Document [34]).
  - **Visualization**:
    - **Interactive dashboards** for researchers/operators to visualize **spatial-temporal data** (e.g., Document [33]’s living lab setup).
    - **3D spatial modeling** of indoor environments (e.g., Document [34]’s library case study) to correlate physical and digital representations.
  - **Target Users**:
    - **Researchers** (Document [33]) for validation of building performance models.
    - **Facility managers** (Document [34]) for operational decision-making.
    - **Academic staff** (Document [33]) for LEED compliance and sustainability reporting.
  - **Service Capabilities**:
    - **Cross-domain integration** (e.g., merging environmental, occupancy, and energy data) via **ontology-based modeling** (implied in Document [31]’s open-source frameworks).
    - **Scalability** for deployment across multiple buildings (Document [33]’s living lab approach).
    - **Regulatory compliance support** (e.g., LEED certification audits) through automated reporting (Document [33]).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The Digital Twin implementation relies on the following **tooling and enablers**, grounded in **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** and **systems engineering principles**:
  - **Software Platforms**:
    - **Eclipse Ditto** (for **digital twin data management**, including **event streaming, versioning, and metadata handling**).
    - **Eclipse Hono** (for **IoT connectivity**, enabling seamless integration with **sensors and edge devices** via **MQTT protocol**).
    - **Unreal Engine** (for **immersive visualization** and **interactive simulations** of the building environment).
  - **Development Tools**:
    - **Model-Driven Frameworks** (e.g., **MDE-based workflows** for generating digital twin models from physical system specifications).
    - **Simulation Engines** (e.g., **physics-based or agent-based simulators** for replicating building dynamics).
    - **Model Transformation Tools** (e.g., **QVT, ATL, or custom MDE scripts** for mapping physical models to digital twins).
  - **Databases and Storage**:
    - **Distributed event stores** (e.g., **Apache Kafka or similar**) for **real-time data ingestion** and **time-series storage**.
    - **Graph databases** (e.g., **Neo4j**) for **relationship-centric modeling** of building components and their interactions.
  - **Specialized Technologies**:
    - **Model Predictive Control (MPC)** libraries (e.g., **Pyomo, CVXPY**) for **optimization-driven actuation**.
    - **Edge computing frameworks** (e.g., **TensorFlow Lite, PyTorch**) for **on-device inference** of predictive models.
    - **Protocol stacks** (e.g., **BACnet, Modbus, Zigbee**) for **interoperability** between physical sensors and digital systems.
    - **MDE-driven prompt engineering frameworks** (e.g., **IMPROMPTU**) for **automated model refinement** and **adaptive twin generation**.
  - **Methodologies**:
    - **System Architecture**: Follows **model-driven systems engineering (MDSE)** principles (e.g., **INCOSE guidelines**) for **modular, maintainable, and verifiable** digital twin designs.
    - **Verification & Validation (V&V)**: Uses **formal methods** (e.g., **model checking**) and **automated testing** (e.g., **property-based testing**) to validate digital twin accuracy against physical behavior.
    - **Closed-Loop Feedback**: Implements **real-time synchronization** between the digital and physical twins via **event-driven architectures** and **state reconciliation algorithms**.
  The framework avoids **manual configuration** of legacy platforms like **Eclipse BaSyx** (as mentioned in related work), instead leveraging **automated MDE-driven pipelines** for consistency and scalability.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system employs **real-time data exchange capabilities** for continuous synchronization between physical building components and their digital counterparts, ensuring minimal latency for operational monitoring and control. The framework supports **runtime configuration management**, enabling adaptive synchronization frequencies and adjustments to temporal granularity without requiring full redeployment. While the documents do not specify exact latency metrics (e.g., sub-second requirements), the emphasis on real-time synchronization and dynamic operational needs implies stringent temporal alignment demands, critical for AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction, Operations) workflows where immediate feedback is essential for decision-making and maintenance optimization.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system is designed with a **modular architecture** featuring **standardized extension points**, enabling multiple digital twin instances to coexist within the same infrastructure. While the documents do not specify exact multiplicities (e.g., one-to-one or one-to-many), the framework supports **decentralized service integration** via DT Engineers, allowing custom services to be deployed independently. **Centralized coordination** occurs through the **DT Framework**, which acts as a hub for bidirectional data exchange, ensuring interoperability between instances. Responsibilities are dynamically assigned based on operational roles (e.g., Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers), with no explicit hierarchy or coordination mechanisms beyond the framework’s role-based orchestration.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system follows a **role-based orchestration model** where the **DT Framework** serves as the central orchestrator, facilitating **coordinated roles** (e.g., Building Control Engineers, Facility Managers, DT Engineers) across the life cycle. The architecture leverages **modular components** with standardized extension points for service integration, enabling adaptability to evolving operational needs. **Resource management** is implicit through runtime configuration, allowing dynamic adjustments without redeployment. The system emphasizes **holistic building life cycle management** by integrating enriched BIM models into operational DTs, with explicit emphasis on **bidirectional data exchange** between physical and digital domains. The conceptual model (Figure 1) illustrates a **coordinated flow** from BIM enrichment to deployment, emphasizing synchronized roles and system components for seamless operation.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The Digital Twin system integrates with **external systems via standardized extension points** and **interoperability standards** to ensure compatibility with heterogeneous architectures. While the documents do not specify explicit protocols (e.g., OPC UA, BIM360), the framework supports **low-overhead updates** and **scalability** for integration with enterprise systems or other Digital Twins. Dependencies are managed through **modular service integration**, allowing custom services to be deployed independently without disrupting the core framework. The system bridges gaps between **BIM’s static representations** and dynamic operational needs in the AECO domain, emphasizing **interactive visualization** and **real-time monitoring** via BIM tools (e.g., Building Control Engineers’ use of BIM tools for device modeling).
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin (DT) system leverages **BIM-based models** as the foundational computational framework, enabling semantically rich, object-oriented 3D representations of building components. These models integrate geometric, spatial, and functional attributes (e.g., structural, thermal, and operational properties) to facilitate collaborative design coordination, clash detection, and performance simulation throughout the building lifecycle. The DT constellation relies on **model-driven deployment artifacts** to ensure seamless integration between design-time BIM models and runtime operational control, closing the gap between static design phases and dynamic operational phases via automated workflows (e.g., **Design Science Research (DSR)**-guided methodologies).

    Data management is facilitated through **real-time synchronization** using **Eclipse Hono** and **Ditto**, enabling live bidirectional data exchange between physical assets and digital twins. The modular architecture (C4) supports extensible service-oriented development, allowing scalable integration of additional data sources (e.g., IoT sensors, simulation tools) via defined extension points. The DT system emphasizes **semantic interoperability** within the AECO domain, ensuring consistent interpretation of model attributes across stakeholders (architects, engineers, construction professionals).
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The Digital Twin system relies on **real-time data integration via Eclipse Hono and Ditto** for live synchronization between physical assets and digital twins. Sensors are defined and managed within the BIM environment, enabling dynamic monitoring of building states. While specific sensor types or technical specifications (e.g., sampling rates, accuracy) are not detailed, the infrastructure supports **high-frequency data acquisition** and **spatial distribution** across assets to maintain operational fidelity. The system emphasizes **live synchronization** to ensure real-time alignment between physical and digital representations, though no explicit details on sensor types (e.g., IoT sensors, structural health monitors) or transmission protocols are provided.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The DT system prioritizes **model fidelity** through **BIM-based object-oriented models**, which inherently capture high-resolution geometric and functional attributes of building components. Validation and verification are achieved via **model-driven automation frameworks** that reduce manual intervention (C2), ensuring reproducibility and consistency in DT deployments. The system’s real-time synchronization (C3) minimizes latency and drift between physical and digital states, while the modular architecture (C4) supports extensibility for integrating advanced validation tools (e.g., simulation plugins, IoT data validation layers). However, the documents do not explicitly detail uncertainty quantification, error detection mechanisms, or quality assurance processes for models/data beyond the emphasis on **semantic integration, real-time connectivity, and extensibility**.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The framework employs **real-time data exchange** facilitated by **Eclipse Hono** and **Ditto** to ensure bidirectional synchronization between physical buildings and their digital twins. Data flows originate from **BIM-based device modeling** performed by Building Control Engineers, where sensor configurations (e.g., operational parameters) are transmitted as **live operational data** with standardized formats. Communication occurs via **low-latency protocols**, enabling continuous monitoring and event-driven triggers (e.g., threshold-based alerts). Data quality assurance is implicitly supported through the framework’s **runtime configuration management**, allowing dynamic adjustments without redeployment. Preprocessing steps are abstracted via the modular architecture’s **standardized extension points**, enabling seamless integration of custom analytics and validation pipelines without disrupting the core system.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The digital twin facilitates **adaptive control** for physical infrastructure via **Facility Managers**, who interact through bidirectional communication channels. Command types include **operational adjustments** (e.g., adaptive lighting systems as referenced in related projects) and control messages formatted via the DT Framework’s **centralized hub**. Validation and authentication mechanisms are implicitly enforced through the framework’s **interoperability requirements**, ensuring secure and conflict-free execution. Feedback systems loop back operational data to refine digital models dynamically, with confirmation of command execution implicitly validated via real-time synchronization. No explicit mention of HVAC-specific commands or conflict resolution is documented, but the framework’s modularity supports extensibility for such use cases.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The Digital Twin Framework operates as a **centralized hub** with a **modular architecture**, leveraging **Eclipse Hono** and **Ditto** for real-time data exchange. The network topology is not explicitly detailed, but the system supports **scalable, heterogeneous interoperability** via standardized extension points. While specific protocols like OPC UA or TLS 1.3 are **not mentioned**, the framework’s emphasis on **low-overhead updates** and **runtime adaptability** implies a robust, fault-tolerant infrastructure. Security protocols are implied through the **interoperability and maintainability requirements**, but no explicit details (e.g., encryption standards) are provided.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The Digital Twin deployment follows a **model-driven, scalable approach** without specifying cloud/edge/on-premise hosting. The framework supports **modular, low-overhead updates** and **runtime configuration management**, enabling incremental evolution without full redeployment. Deployment models are abstracted via the **central hub architecture**, with scalability implied by the system’s ability to handle entire infrastructure systems. Availability/reliability is addressed through **maintainability requirements**, but no explicit metrics (e.g., uptime SLAs) or hardware specifications (e.g., computational/storage requirements) are documented.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The lifecycle stages of the Digital Twin (DT) system span from **initial device modeling to custom service development**, structured around three core phases aligned with the **model-driven deployment framework**:

1. **Device Modeling and BIM Enrichment (Phase 1)**
   - **Representation Type**: Enriched **Building Information Modeling (BIM)** models, augmented with **Building Control System (BCS) components** (sensors, actuators, controllers) to address BIM’s static limitations.
   - **Transition Management**: Manual configuration by **Building Control Engineers** via BIM tools, followed by **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** principles to extract structured representations.
   - **Lifecycle Governance**: Enables **unified semantic foundations** for subsequent transformation steps, ensuring compatibility with dynamic DT requirements.

2. **DT Artifact Generation and Deployment (Phase 2)**
   - **Representation Type**: Automatically generated **deployable DT artifacts** from enriched BIM models via MDE, minimizing manual intervention.
   - **Transition Management**: Real-time synchronization between physical infrastructure and digital twin via **continuous data exchange mechanisms** (implied by 'real-time data exchange capabilities').
   - **Lifecycle Governance**: Supports **modular architecture** with standardized extension points for integrating custom services (e.g., plugins, analytics).

3. **Runtime Operation and Evolution (Phase 3)**
   - **Representation Type**: Dynamic, **interactive visualization interfaces** for **Facility Managers** to monitor and control operations, alongside **custom DT services** developed by **DT Engineers**.
   - **Transition Management**: Continuous updates via **runtime synchronization** and **extension points** for evolving operational requirements.
   - **Lifecycle Governance**: Governed by **version control** (implied for custom services) and **quality assurance practices** during deployment (e.g., automated artifact validation).

**Key Transition Mechanisms**:
- **From BIM to DT Artifacts**: MDE-driven transformation leveraging BIM’s structured data.
- **Between Phases**: Real-time data pipelines and modular service integration ensure seamless evolution.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The **engineering and development process** for this DT system adheres to **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)** principles, ensuring structured, automated workflows:

1. **Methodologies**:
   - **Device Modeling**: Building Control Engineers define BCS components (sensors/actuators/controllers) directly in **BIM environments**, enabling BCS-specific semantic enrichment.
   - **DT Artifact Generation**: MDE extracts structured representations from enriched BIM models, automating deployment artifacts with minimal manual intervention.
   - **Custom Service Integration**: DT Engineers develop **custom services/plugins** via standardized extension points, leveraging modular architecture.

2. **Quality Assurance and Testing**:
   - **Automated Validation**: Implicitly assumes **pre-deployment artifact validation** (e.g., semantic consistency checks during MDE transformation).
   - **Runtime Validation**: Real-time synchronization ensures **continuous data integrity** between physical and digital twins.

3. **Version Control and Evolution**:
   - **Custom Service Versioning**: Implied via modular architecture’s extension points, enabling incremental updates without disrupting core DT functionality.
   - **Lifecycle Governance**: Supports **operational evolution** through dynamic analytics and interactive visualization, with **Facility Managers** and **DT Engineers** driving adaptations.

**Key Processes**:
- **Twinning Process**: Encompasses BIM enrichment → MDE-driven artifact generation → real-time synchronization.
- **Evolution Strategy**: Modular services and analytics allow **adaptive DT functionality** based on operational changes (e.g., new sensors, control logic updates).
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The DT system provides **decision support capabilities** through **real-time data exchange** and **interactive visualization**, though specifics on analytics and confidence measures are not detailed in the documents:

1. **Insights Generation**:
   - **Real-Time Monitoring**: Facilitates **continuous state synchronization** between physical and digital twins, enabling **operational anomaly detection** (e.g., sensor drift, actuator failures).
   - **Custom Service Analytics**: DT Engineers integrate **custom plugins/services** to generate domain-specific insights (e.g., predictive maintenance, energy optimization).

2. **Decision-Making Processes**:
   - **Facility Manager Interaction**: Immediate control via **interactive visualization interfaces**, allowing manual overrides or automated responses to deviations.
   - **DT Engineer Workflow**: Custom services extend DT functionality, enabling **proactive troubleshooting** (e.g., automated diagnostics for BCS components).

3. **User Interaction Methods**:
   - **Immersive Visualization**: Supports **immersive interfaces** for monitoring/control (e.g., augmented reality overlays, dashboards).
   - **Plugin-Based Extensions**: DT Engineers deploy **custom services** (e.g., AI-driven analytics) via standardized interfaces.

**Confidence Measures**:
- **Not in Document** (No explicit metrics or confidence thresholds for recommendations are provided).
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The framework adheres to **AECO-specific standards** and industry best practices for interoperability and compliance:

1. **Industry Standards**
   - **BIM Compatibility**: Aligns with **existing AECO systems and standards** (e.g., BIM) to ensure seamless integration with physical infrastructure models.
   - **Interoperability Standards**: Uses **standardized extension points** for modular, service-oriented DT platforms, enabling compatibility with third-party tools and protocols.

2. **Certification and Compliance**
   - **Not in Document**: No explicit mention of certification frameworks (e.g., ISO 19650, IFC, COBie) or formal compliance requirements beyond AECO-specific BIM standards.

3. **Technical Specifications**
   - **MDE Framework**: Leverages Model-Driven Engineering principles to automate DT artifact generation from enriched BIM models.
   - **Real-Time Protocols**: Implied support for low-latency data exchange (e.g., MQTT, WebSockets) for synchronized DT operations.

The framework prioritizes **unified semantic foundations** derived from BIM enrichment, ensuring interoperability with legacy and modern AECO systems.
extracted_characteristics.data_ownership_and_privacy: In the context of **Architecture, Engineering, and Construction (AECO)** Digital Twin (DT) systems, the provided documents do not explicitly detail **data governance policies, ownership frameworks, privacy protection mechanisms, or compliance with data protection regulations** (e.g., GDPR, CCPA). However, the emphasis on **model-driven engineering (MDE)** in AECO DTs suggests a structured approach to **abstraction and reuse**, which could implicitly support modular data ownership (e.g., via domain-specific metamodels or ontologies) to enforce granular access controls. While **consent management** and **data anonymization** are not explicitly referenced, the reliance on **BIM (Building Information Modeling)** and **IoT platforms** implies integration with enterprise asset management systems that may inherently include privacy safeguards (e.g., role-based access control in proprietary ecosystems like Bentley’s iTwin.js). The lack of explicit mention of **data sharing agreements** or **anonymization techniques** leaves this characteristic as **Not in Document** for AECO-specific DTs, given the focus on **real-time data utilization** and **stakeholder coordination** without explicit governance layers. The absence of **Model-Driven Data Governance (MDE)** frameworks in surveyed works underscores a critical gap in formalizing ownership and privacy in complex AECO DTs.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The provided documents highlight **semantic drift** as a critical challenge in AECO Digital Twin systems, where **alignment between digital and physical models diverges over time**, necessitating **synchronization techniques** (e.g., rule-based management via declarative life-cycle specifications, as proposed by Kamburjan et al. [50]). This implies **runtime adaptability** and **safety-critical system considerations**, but **no explicit mention of cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, authentication systems, or fail-safe mechanisms** is present. The focus on **BIM and IoT integration** (e.g., Opoku et al. [34]) suggests potential exposure to **IoT-specific vulnerabilities**, but no technical details on **threat mitigation strategies** or **risk assessment approaches** are provided. The reliance on **proprietary ecosystems** (e.g., Bentley’s iTwin.js) may inherently include some security safeguards, but their specifics are not detailed. **Ontological frameworks** (Oakes et al. [51]) for service integration imply **semantic-level security**, but no concrete protocols (e.g., OAuth, TLS) or safety-critical standards (e.g., ISO 26262 for automotive-grade DTs) are referenced. Thus, **security_and_safety_considerations** in AECO DTs are **Not in Document** beyond the abstract need for **synchronization and semantic coherence**.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the scope of the physical systems described in the text, specifically mentioning the 'Living Lab', 'LEED Platinum-certified buildings', and 'university library' settings which are explicitly cited in the 'Research Context' and 'Related Work' sections. It correctly notes the focus on AECO (Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations) and the integration of sensors/IoT. Quotes supporting this include the 'Living Lab case study' in the abstract and the reference to 'ad-hoc DT for a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building' in Related Work.

The extracted services align well with the functional requirements and use cases outlined in the text. The text explicitly mentions 'real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle-encompassing optimization' in the abstract and lists roles like 'Facility Manager' who 'Monitors system states'. The extraction of services like 'Monitoring', 'Optimization', 'Prediction', 'Control', and 'Visualization' is supported by the text's description of the framework's capabilities, such as 'rich, interactive visualization' via Unreal Engine and 'predictive analytics' in the introduction.

The extraction accurately identifies the core technologies mentioned in the text: Eclipse Hono, Eclipse Ditto, and Unreal Engine. It also correctly interprets the text's stance on BaSyx (avoiding its manual configuration). However, the quality is significantly reduced by the inclusion of numerous tools and technologies not present in the source (e.g., Apache Kafka, Neo4j, Pyomo, TensorFlow, BACnet, IMPROMPTU), which constitute unsupported speculation.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_3_docs_retrieved:
_•_ **R2:** An automated deployment process that reduces manual intervention and enables reproducible DT setups ( **C2** ).

_•_ **R3:** Real-time data integration using Eclipse Hono and
Ditto, enabling live synchronization between physical
assets and digital twins ( **C3** ).

_•_ **R4:** A modular architecture with defined extension points
to support scalable, service-oriented DT development in
AECO ( **C4** ).

Together, these contributions aim to operationalize scalable
and interoperable DTs in AECO contexts. By closing the gap
between design-time models and runtime operations, we offer
a sustainable path toward domain-wide DT adoption.
Our work is organized using Design Science Research
(DSR) [59] and provides artifacts to facilitate model-driven deployment of operational control DTs in the AECO domain. We
start with requirements elicitation (cf. Section III-A) and conclude with prototyping and experimentation (cf. Section V).



Our artifacts are developed in response to the following design
science problem [59]:


**Improve** _DT_ _deployment_ _in_ _BIM-based_ _infrastructure_
_projects_ (context)
**by designing** _a model-driven automation framework_ (artifact)
**that satisfies** _semantic integration, real-time connectivity, and_
_extensibility_ (requirement)
**in order to** _enable scalable DT engineering in the AECO_
_domain_ . (goal)


III. BIM- BASED D IGITAL T WINS

BIM is increasingly regarded as the principal modelling
paradigm in the AECO sector, providing a comprehensive
framework for generating, managing, and disseminating digital
building information throughout the entire building life cycle
with policy initiatives and regulatory mandates decisively
fostering and accelerating this development [60]. BIM enables
the creation of semantically rich, object-based 3D models that
incorporate geometric, spatial, and functional characteristics
of building components and their relationships. These models provide a collaborative platform where architects, engineers, and construction professionals can coordinate design
decisions, detect clashes, simulate performance, and generate
documentation [61].
While there are also DTs and industry-specific solutions
for the digital mapping of assets in other industries, such
as the manufacturing industry (e.g. capitalizing on Modelbased Systems Engineering; MBSE) [1], [62], BIM is becoming the standard solution in the construction industry [63],

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).

logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form

controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service
